posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked: A group of 10 of us has rented a house in Rully early next May, and we’ll be doing some wine tasting as well. All of us are wine-savvy, and I think the etiquette is pretty good across the group. I know the bigger houses (Drouhin, Bruno Clair) can easily accommodate us, but I wasn’t sure if smaller domains would be able to. Before I directly asked them (I have a number in mind within an hour or so of Rully that I already purchase wine from and that I’d like to turn others onto), I wanted to check with this board to make sure that I wasn’t committing some sort of mortal faux pas by asking them to deal with 10 of us. What say you?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Not a faux pas, just unlikely many will say yes (including, I would have thought, Clair, which isn’t really a big house) as it’s a bigger group than most estates are adapted for.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: I’ve been specifically told by a number of Domaines not to bring more than 6 when there was a chance a couple friends from Paris might join us on an impromptu basis.

posts.name: Larry Stein
posts.cooked: When I was there in 2014, 3 of us had an appointment at Domaine des Lambrays. When we arrived there was a group of 8-10 folks from Texas with the same appointment time. Obviously, that was pre-pandemic so I don’t know if the policy has changed.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Larry_Stein:

When I was there in 2014, 3 of us had an appointment at Domaine des Lambrays. When we arrived there was a group of 8-10 folks from Texas with the same appointment time. Obviously, that was pre-pandemic so I don’t know if the policy has changed.

In the context of my comment, I’m sure it’s Domaine specific! But there are some Domaines (even fairly large ones) where groups visits of more than 8 people would be somewhat impractical given the sizes of the cellars (and how the barrels are stacked) and the attention they want to devote to each visit. I’m sure some can handle it!

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Apart from the physical constraints, I think if you show up as a group of 10, you’re likely to be viewed many places as tourists and not serious wine people. Just a hunch.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
John_Morris:

Apart from the physical constraints, I think if you show up as a group of 10, you’re likely to be viewed many places as tourists and not serious wine people. Just a hunch.

That’s my fear as well, though to be clear I wouldn’t just be showing up.

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked: We just did 8 all over Champagne & Burgundy. It was 2 industry folks (US importer/retailer) and 6 “guests.” All visits were set up with places that they import or sell (via the importer). Never felt like our group was too large, though we did have to walk out to the stairwell at Louis Michel and grab an extra chair…

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked: Well, you are likely to need to split the group for transportation. Why not make successive appointments?

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
maureen_nelson:

Well, you are likely to need to split the group for transportation. Why not make successive appointments?

We won’t have to split the group actually. Have a mini bus driving us everywhere! But good thought otherwise on the split appointments.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
HoosJustinG:

We just did 8 all over Champagne & Burgundy. It was 2 industry folks (US importer/retailer) and 6 “guests.” All visits were set up with places that they import or sell (via the importer). Never felt like our group was too large, though we did have to walk out to the stairwell at Louis Michel and grab an extra chair…

It’s different when there’s someone in the trade.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked: Thanks all for the thoughtful responses. As always, this community is incredibly helpful.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked: Did you end up booking anything yet? I only have a group of 2, but so far everyone’s politely declined.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked: who did you ask?

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked:
Admittedly, I know very little about Burgundy and my limited experience drinking it is all over the place, but I’ve been trying to look at William’s reviews/articles and posts on this board to help guide me. Also, I realize I’ve made things harder since my only full day there is a Saturday (between my time in Champagne and N. Rhone).

I’ve sent nice emails and received polite “sorry, we’re not available / don’t offer tastings to public” from: Domaine Hubert Lignier, Domaine Arlaud (these two seemed to just be away/busy that day), Domaine Mugneret-Gibourg, Dujac, and Domaine Arnoux-Lachaux. I wasn’t expecting a positive (or any) response from the last three, but figured I’d try.

I’m thinking of maybe asking Barthod (but can’t find their email address), Bruno Clair, and/or Roblet-Monnot, but I’m very much out of my depth here and open to suggestions.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
J_Rock:

Admittedly, I know very little about Burgundy and my limited experience drinking it is all over the place, but I’ve been trying to look at William’s reviews/articles and posts on this board to help guide me. Also, I realize I’ve made things harder since my only full day there is a Saturday (between my time in Champagne and N. Rhone).

I’ve sent nice emails and received polite “sorry, we’re not available / don’t offer tastings to public” from: Domaine Hubert Lignier, Domaine Arlaud (these two seemed to just be away/busy that day), Domaine Mugneret-Gibourg, Dujac, and Domaine Arnoux-Lachaux. I wasn’t expecting a positive (or any) response from the last three, but figured I’d try.

I’m thinking of maybe asking Barthod (but can’t find their email address), Bruno Clair, and/or Roblet-Monnot, but I’m very much out of my depth here and open to suggestions.

Saturday is difficult generally for visits, but you’ve also made some fairly ambitious requests. Without help, those are usually difficult. I can’t speak as to Bruno Clair, but Barthod has usually been a tough visit to arrange.

posts.name: Jordan_Rock
posts.cooked:
Yeah, some (e.g., Lachaux, Dujac) I didn’t even expect to respond, but La Revue du Vin de France says the others do tastings by appointment. Either way, I’m not holding my breath, but figure there’s no harm in sending a nice email just in case.

Do you have any suggestions for exciting/good intimate tastings that are less ambitious or more realistic?

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Hi J-Rock.

All of the places you are trying to get into would get many, many requests per day for visits. Most probably will only see their importer and or press these days. These are farmers who juggle time between the vineyards and the cellars and are of limited resource.

Your best bet is to go to some of the better Negociants, who are set up to receive visitors to taste. Olivier Leflaive, Bouchard Pere et Fils, Louis Jadot and likes should be where you focus your energy.

kind regards
Jeremy

posts.name: Matt Mauldin
posts.cooked:
I’ve had luck in the past by doing a combination of things, with very enjoyable results.

Typically I’ll start by researching to find producers who are under the radar, up-and-coming, or who otherwise look interesting. With this I was able to see Lachaux in 2016 right before the Domaine’s popularity skyrocketed, among many other enjoyable visits (Genot-Boulanger is another good example). Next, I’ll choose a few unicorns to target, with the goal of landing one of them. And knowing that some are just not going to be realistic. I’ve had luck at the level of Domaine de Lambrays & Meo-Camuzet (staying at the latter’s Air BnB helped…), both very enjoyable visits. Then, I’ll fill the gaps with quality Negociants. Drouhin has a cool tour. Bouchard has a nice Premier/Grand Cru tasting. Olivier Leflaive had a restaurant with lunch or dinner pairings.

I work in the wine industry, so that can help, but I’m not a big spender with any type of clout, so I don’t have that in my favor. I’ve never gotten help to get an appointment in Burgundy, always DIY. Writing in French helps. Making it a ‘learning-based’ theme or otherwise demonstrating some passion or thirst for knowledge helps. Flexibility helps. As others have said, you’re not going to have much luck on a Saturday… but maybe next time…

Same approach has worked very well in the Northern and Southern Rhone, and was a home run in Barolo.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
J_Rock:

Yeah, some (e.g., Lachaux, Dujac) I didn’t even expect to respond, but La Revue du Vin de France says the others do tastings by appointment. Either way, I’m not holding my breath, but figure there’s no harm in sending a nice email just in case.

Do you have any suggestions for exciting/good intimate tastings that are less ambitious or more realistic?

Well, all producers do visits/tastings, it’s a matter of getting in. You’re absolutely right there’s no harm in sending an email, but the way to get visits is have some kind of connection - you’ve met them, drink a lot of their wines, know their importer, know a distributor, etc… The personal connection (or someone else’s personal connection) is the key, so I would leverage those.

Jeremy’s advice regarding the negoce houses is very good - both Drouhin and Bouchard do nice visits, including on Saturday. (I’ve not been to the others.) For small/personal visits, reach out to smaller producers you’ve tried and tell them about the experience. Some may well say yes.

fancy_title: Wine tasting in Burgundy - is 10 people too many?
title: Wine tasting in Burgundy - is 10 people too many?
